coredata.dc:title: Preparation of new full UV-absorbing carbon dots from quercetin and their application in UV-absorbing systems for waterborne polyurethane coatings
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Z.
Huang, W.
Chen, C.
Ye, W.
Guo, B.
Qiu, J.
Zhuang, J.
Hu, C.
Lei, B.
Hu, G.
Liu, Y.
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a kind of novel fully UV absorbing carbon dots (QC-CDs) were prepared as UV absorbers by a one-step hydrothermal method using quercetin as raw material. The prepared QC-CDs have high absorption efficiency (over 98%) in the whole ultraviolet region (200–400 nm), and are almost completely transmitted in the visible light region. The prepared QC-CDs have good stability and resistance to photobleaching. After the treatment with different pH and temperature, the QC-CDs maintained good absorption stability. QC-CDs have a wider UV absorption range than commercial water-soluble organic UV absorbers-BP-4. In terms of aging resistance, QC-CDs can achieve the same effect as BP-4. After subjecting QC-CDs and BP-4 to different pH values, it was found that QC-CDs remain fully UV absorbing at different pH conditions and maintain good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. Then, the UV absorption mechanism of QC-CDs has also been investigated and applied to the UV absorption systems of waterborne coatings PU. Finally, the QC-CDs@PU with full UV absorption properties was applied in the photodegradation prevention experiment of Rhodamine B. At present, the preparation process of QC-CDs is simple and environmentally friendly. QC-CDs have the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: UV full absorption
Water-soluble UV absorbers
Photodegradation prevention

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV absorption stability of QC-CDs and water-soluble organic UV absorber BP-4 under different pH conditions
UV absorption stability and photobleaching resistance of QC-CDs under different temperature conditions
The absorption efficiency of QC-CDs and BP-4
The demonstration of the UV shielding effect of QC-CDs and BP-4
The role of QC-CDs in UV-absorption systems of waterborne coating
The application of QC-CDs@PU in preventing photodegradation of rhodamine B

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of QC-CDs. (c) Size distribution of QC-CDs. (d) Raman spectra of QC-CDs. (e and f) XRD and FTIR spectra of QC-CDs. (g)–(i) XPS survey, C 1s and O 1s spectra.

(a) UV absorption spectra of QC-CDs and Photograph of quercetin was irradiated by a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm (left inset) and Photograph of QC-CDs was irradiated by a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm (right inset). (b) Fluorescence spectra of QC-CDs under different excitations. (c) Normalized fluorescence spectra of QC-CDs. (d) The UV absorption mechanism of QC-CDs (left) and infrared thermal image of pure PU and QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs under ultraviolet radiation (right).

(a) UV absorption spectra of QC-CDs under different pH conditions. (b) The changes in the positions of the three absorption peaks of QC-CDs during the acid-base cycle. (c) The FT-IR spectra of QC-CDs under different pH conditions. (d) UV absorption spectra of BP-4 under different pH conditions. (e) The changes in the positions of the two absorption peaks of BP-4 during the acid-base cycle. (f) The FT-IR spectra of BP-4 under different pH conditions.
(a) UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of QC-CDs under different temperature conditions. (b) UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of the photobleaching resistance of QC-CDs.
(a) Integration of UV–Vis transmission spectra in the 280–400 nm band for BP-4. (b) Integration of UV–Vis transmission spectra in the 280–400 nm band for QC-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of QC-CDs and BP-4. (b) A model demonstrates the UV shielding effect of BP-4 and QC-CDs by using the UV flashlight with a wavelength of 285 nm. (c) Photos of demonstrating the UV shielding effect of BP-4 and QC-CDs using a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm. (d) Photos of demonstrating the UV shielding effect of pure water, BP-4 and QC-CDs using a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 285 nm.

(a) Photos of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU doped with different QC-CDs and BP-4 concentrations. (b) UV–Vis transmission spectra of QC-CDs@PU doped with different QC-CDs concentrations. (c) UV–Vis transmission spectra of BP-4@PU doped with different BP-4 concentrations. (d) The UV absorption efficiencies of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU. (e) The absorption efficiencies of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU at UVA and UVB bands. (f) and (g) are the intuitive comparison of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU for UV–Vis light, respectively.

(a) The rhodamine B solution wrapped by 40 mg/mL QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU under continuous 365 UV irradiation, respectively. (b) Absorption spectra of rhodamine B solution wrapped by QC-CDs@PU at different UV irradiation times. (c) Absorption spectra with a wavelength of 540–565 nm of rhodamine B solution wrapped by QC-CDs@PU at different UV irradiation times. (d) Absorption spectra of rhodamine B solution wrapped by BP-4@PU at different UV irradiation times. (e) Statistical graphs of degradation rates of rhodamine B wrapped by QC-CDs@PU and wrapped by BP-4@PU under different UV irradiation times.
The UV absorption efficiency of QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs in UVA and UVB.
The UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@PU doped with different concentrations of BP-4 in UVA and UVB.

head.ce:title: Preparation of new full UV-absorbing carbon dots from quercetin and their application in UV-absorbing systems for waterborne polyurethane coatings

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biomassed Materias and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biomassed Materias and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biomassed Materias and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a kind of novel fully UV absorbing carbon dots (QC-CDs) were prepared as UV absorbers by a one-step hydrothermal method using quercetin as raw material. The prepared QC-CDs have high absorption efficiency (over 98%) in the whole ultraviolet region (200–400 nm), and are almost completely transmitted in the visible light region. The prepared QC-CDs have good stability and resistance to photobleaching. After the treatment with different pH and temperature, the QC-CDs maintained good absorption stability. QC-CDs have a wider UV absorption range than commercial water-soluble organic UV absorbers-BP-4. In terms of aging resistance, QC-CDs can achieve the same effect as BP-4. After subjecting QC-CDs and BP-4 to different pH values, it was found that QC-CDs remain fully UV absorbing at different pH conditions and maintain good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. Then, the UV absorption mechanism of QC-CDs has also been investigated and applied to the UV absorption systems of waterborne coatings PU. Finally, the QC-CDs@PU with full UV absorption properties was applied in the photodegradation prevention experiment of Rhodamine B. At present, the preparation process of QC-CDs is simple and environmentally friendly. QC-CDs have the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, ultraviolet light is one of the natural components of sunlight. According to the different wavelengths, UV light can be divided into three parts: UVC (200–280 nm), UVB (280–320 nm) and UVA (320–400 nm). Among them, UVC will be absorbed by the ozone layer, and only UVB and UVA can break through the ozone layer to reach the Earth's surface [1–3]. UVB is more destructive to polymer materials, which will lead to the generation of free radicals in polymer, thus destroying the internal binding energy of the polymer, gradually decomposing polymer materials, and lowering its mechanical properties [4]. As for UVA, although the sensitive wavelengths of most polymers are not in the UVA range, UVA is very harmful to the human body, it can penetrate into the human dermis and destroy the skin elastic fibers, causing skin relaxation and wrinkles, and in serious cases, it can also induce skin cancer [5–7]. Furthermore, this part of the ultraviolet light will also lead to the decomposition and fading of dyes [8].

There are two kinds of commercially available UV absorbers, organic UV absorbers and inorganic UV absorbers [9]. Organic UV absorbers mainly include salicylic acid, benzophenone, benzotriazole, triazine and other organic compounds. Their commonality is that they all contain conjugated π-π∗ electronic systems. Most of them are oil-soluble, while water-soluble is almost only soluble in alcohol. There are two commercially available types that can be dissolved in water, 2-hydroxy-4-methoxy benzophenone-5-sulfonic acid (BP-4) and 2-phenylbenzimi-dazole-5-sulfonic acid (UV-T). This type of UV absorber has some disadvantages, such as narrow absorption range, toxicity and expensiveness [4,5], etc. Inorganic UV absorbers mainly include zinc oxide, titanium dioxide and other metal oxides [10–12]. This type of UV absorber has some disadvantages, such as low absorption efficiency, poor permeability, low light transmittance, poor dispersibility and high cost [13,14], etc. Moreover, this type of UV shielding agent has poor affinity with organic materials, and it is difficult to blend into the polymer materials [5,12].

Quercetin is a kind of flavonoid widely distributed in the plant kingdom and it can complex or capture free radicals to prevent lipid peroxidation in vivo. It is widely found in the stems, skins, flowers, leaves, buds, seeds and fruits of many plants. Among them, the content is higher in the stems and leaves of buckwheat, sea-buckthorn, hawthorn and onions. Quercetin is cheap and simple to extract, and can be obtained from nature in a constant stream.

CDs are sphere-like nanoparticles with particle size less than 10 nm [15–22], which have size limiting effect and photoluminescence properties [23–26]. They can emit visible light under UV excitation, so they have UV absorption properties [4,27–29]. The studies published in the last five years on the use of carbon dots as UV absorbers are counted in Table S1. As far as the available published literature is concerned, no one has yet synthesized UV fully absorbing carbon quantum dots as UV absorbers using a single molecule as raw material. And research on the synthesis of carbon dots using biomass as raw material and using them as ultraviolet absorption is very rare. At present, only Li et al. have prepared biomass CQDs from waste acorn cups and explore their potential applications as UV absorbent in the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite film [4]. However, as far as the ultraviolet absorption, this carbon dots not only can't absorb all UV light, but also seriously affect the transmission of visible light. In terms of UV absorption mechanisms, due to its size-limiting effects and photoluminescence properties, CDs can emit visible light under UV excitation. When CDs are exposed to UV light, the energy it absorbs is dissipated in the form of emission of longer wavelength light. Barman et al. incorporated N-doped carbon dots N-CDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid and ethylenediamine into PVA [11]. Since the N-CDs were encapsulated in a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, the absolute QY was as high as 91% and the UV light energy absorbed by N-CDs is dissipated in the form of long-wavelength light radiation. Patila et al. used WTR-CDs prepared from waste tea residue powder as raw material to incorporate into PVA films and the composite films were able to not only block the UV region but also re-emit the absorbed UV into the visible region [30]. There are many other studies in this category [31,32], and due to their high quantum efficiency, the energy absorbed by ultraviolet light is dissipated in the form of optical radiation. However, the PLQY of the QC-CDs in this study was only 4.9%. Therefore, the photoconversion mechanism is different and it was demonstrated by infrared thermography that QC-CDs absorb high energy of short-wavelength ultraviolet light and disperse in the form of thermal energy.

In this study, QC-CDs were prepared from quercetin and it not only absorbs all UV light but also doesn't affect the transmission of visible light. The prepared QC-CDs have good stability and resistance to photobleaching. Compared with the commercial water-soluble organic UV absorber BP-4, QC-CDs have a wider UV absorption range and it is comparable to BP-4 in terms of anti-aging. After subjecting QC-CDs and BP-4 to different pH values, it was found that QC-CDs remain fully UV absorbing at different pH conditions and maintain good absorption stability, but BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. The UV absorption mechanism of QC-CDs has also been investigated and applied to the UV absorption systems of waterborne coatings PU. At present, the preparation process of QC-CDs is simple, environmentally friendly [33]; it has the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers.

Quercetin (C15H10O7, 98%) and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Industries, Inc. 2-hydroxy-4-methoxy benzophenone-5-sulfonic acid (BP-4) (C14H12O6S, ≥98%) was purchased from Taobao, Polyurethane (PU) was purchased from YuanYe Industries, Inc. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%) and concentrated sulfuric acid anhydrous (the quality score is 98%) were purchased from Macklin Industries, Inc.

A new type of all-ultraviolet absorbing carbon dots (QC-CDs) was synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method with quercetin as raw material. Quercetin (0.016 g) and ethanol (20 mL) were sealed in a steel autoclave with a PTFE liner and placed in an oven for 6 h at 180 °C. Waiting for the solution to cool to room temperature, the reaction solution was filtered through an aqueous needle filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Then using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 500 to dialyze it for 36 h and drying it under vacuum at 75 °C, and finally the concentrate was freeze-dried to obtain gray solid QC-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of QC-CDs were measured with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi). The XRD profiles of QC-CDs were measured with a Persee XD-2X/M4600 (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.154051 nm). The elemental percentages of QC-CDs and XPS images were detected using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos). The absolute PLQY of QC-CDs were measured using an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer and solvent ethanol as a blank sample as QY standard. The FT-IR spectra of QC-CDs were measured with a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer with KBr pellet as a reference ranging from 450 to 4000 cm−1. TEM images and HRTEM images of QC-CDs were obtained using a JEOL2010 electron microscope. Infrared thermography photographs of the slides were recorded with FLIR (H16, Hikvision). The fluorescence spectral profiles of QC-CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi).

Under continuous stirring condition, take 10 mL of commercial aqueous coating (PU) in a beaker, then add the pre-prepared QC-CDs solution and continue to stir until it is mixed well, then apply it to the slide and dry it in an oven at 60 °C to get a QC-CDs@PU sample, and the fabricated QC-CDs@PU doped with different QC-CDs contents were stored in dry containers.

The synthesized QC-CDs were first characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM photographs confirmed that the resulting QC-CDs had good dispersion in aqueous solution (Fig. 1 a) with an average particle size of approximately 4.0 nm (Fig. 1c) and the TEM images of QC-CDs with sizes of 50 nm, 20 nm, 2 nm and 1 nm, respectively, are shown in Fig. S1. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images showed 0.21 nm lattice stripes, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite (Fig. 1b) [4,34]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows two broad characteristic absorption bands at 1380 cm−1 (D-band) and 1574 cm−1 (G-band) respectively, with the characteristic absorption band at 1380 cm−1 (D band) indicates the existence of a C–C bond of sp3 character [9,11,34] and the characteristic absorption band at 1574 cm−1 (G-band) indicating the existence of C=C of sp2 character [9,11,34], where ID/IG = 0.77, indicating a high degree of carbonization. The XRD patterns of quercetin and QC-CDs are given in Fig. 1e. There is a significant difference in the XRD patterns between quercetin and QC-CDs, with the characteristic diffraction peak of QC-CDs at 2θ = 42.4°, corresponding to the graphite (100) plane [1,34], while quercetin shows no peak at 2θ = 42.4°. However, quercetin has three sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 10.7°, 12.6° and 26.4°, respectively. This result indicates that the original crystalline form of quercetin was destroyed and graphitization increased after high temperature carbonization, which is consistent with the Raman and TEM results.

The chemical composition and surface functional groups of the synthesized QC-CDs were further investigated by FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1f, there is a significant difference in FT-IR patterns between quercetin and QC-CDs. The surface functional groups of QC-CDs are very simple, it just has three absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1, 1650 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH, C=O and the aromatic ring C=C [1,4,11,33,34], respectively. However, FT-IR spectra of quercetin show that quercetin has an abundance of functional groups. The absorption peak at 3430 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, 1480 cm−1, 1250 cm−1and 780 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O [35,36], the aromatic ring C=C [1,4,11,34], C–O–C and the benzene ring, respectively, which shows all the functional groups carried by quercetin. The XPS full-scan spectra and the high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s of QC-CDs are listed in Fig. 1(g-i). The XPS full-scan spectra show that the QC-CDs are mainly composed of C and O elements with binding energies of 284.49 eV and 530.17 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1g). The percentages of C and O atoms are 70.54% and 29.46%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1h) shows the presence of C=C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.6 eV) and C=O (287.6 eV) [1,4,33,34] on the surface of QC-CDs. The O1s high-resolution spectrum can be fitted with two peaks at 532.2 eV and 533.1 eV [1,4,33,34] (Fig. 1i), which are attributed to C–O and C=O, respectively. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy are consistent with the FT-IR results, which indicate that QC-CDs contain –OH, aromatic rings and C=O functional groups on the surface.

Fig. 2 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of quercetin and QC-CDs, respectively, and it can be seen that there is a significant difference between the UV–Vis absorption spectra of quercetin and QC-CDs. Quercetin has two absorption peaks at 253 nm and 362 nm, respectively. The absorption peak at 253 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ leap of the benzene ring C=C bond [37–39] and the absorption peak at 362 nm is attributed to the n-π∗ leap of the C=O bond [38,40,41] and we can prove this from the infrared spectrum of quercetin in Fig. 1f. However, there are three absorption peaks of QC-CDs, which are located at 261 nm, 290 nm and 362 nm, respectively. The absorption peak at 261 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ leap of the benzene ring C=C bond attached to the surface of the carbon core. Compared with the π-π∗ leap of the benzene ring C=C in quercetin, it has some red shift which is caused by the large π-conjugation of carbon core. While the absorption peak at 290 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ leap of the aromatic cluster attached to the surface of the carbon core, and the absorption peak at 362 nm is attributed to the n-π∗ leap of the C=O bond. We can prove this from the simple infrared spectrum and the XPS spectra of QC-CDs in Fig. 1f.

Because of its size-limiting effect and photoluminescence properties, CDs can emit visible light under UV excitation (inset of Fig. 2a). In Fig. 2b, when QC-CDs are excited by laser with wavelengths of 300–420 nm, it exhibits typical excitation-dependent behavior. When QC-CDs are excited by UV light at 320 nm, it has the strongest blue fluorescence emission at 416 nm and its normalized fluorescence spectrum is shown in Fig. 2c. However, its PLQY is only 4.9%, and most of the energy absorbed by QC-CDs is dissipated in the form of heat energy. In Fig. 2d, one end of the slide was coated with QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs and the middle with pure PU, then placed them in turn under a UV lamp for 1 min and took infrared thermography to record the change in temperature of the slide. The temperature coated with QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs are 33.7 °C, 35.9 °C and 37.4 °C, respectively, while that in the place coated with pure PU is only 32.0 °C, 33.8 °C and 34.5 °C, respectively, demonstrating that most of the energy absorbed by the carbon dots is dispersed in the form of thermal energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV absorption stability of QC-CDs and water-soluble organic UV absorber BP-4 under different pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Since the ultimate purpose of QC-CDs is to serve as water-soluble UV absorbers, the UV absorption stability of QC-CDs under different pH conditions was investigated, and the UV absorption stability of QC-CDs and the commercial water-soluble UV absorber BP-4 was compared under different pH conditions.

Fig. 3 a and d show the UV–Vis absorption spectra of QC-CDs and BP-4 under different pH conditions, respectively. It can be seen that the UV absorption of QC-CDs and BP-4 are unchanged under acidic conditions. While the UV absorption of QC-CDs is red-shifted under alkaline conditions and it still completely absorbs ultraviolet. But BP-4 loses its value as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. This is because of the effect of deprotonation on the UV absorption of QC-CDs and BP-4. In order to prove that the changes of the UV absorption of QC-CDs and BP-4 under alkaline conditions are due to deprotonation, rather than the change of structure or surface functional groups of QC-CDs and BP-4 under alkaline conditions, the acid-base circulation of QC-CDs and BP-4 solutions was carried out for this purpose. Firstly, adjusting the QC-CDs and BP-4 solution to acidity (pH = 2), and then measured its UV–Vis absorption spectrum. Then, the acidic QC-CDs and BP-4 solution was adjusted to alkalinity (pH = 12), and then measured its UV–Vis absorption spectrum. According to this method, the acid-base circulation was carried out 3 times, and the UV–Vis absorption spectrums during the process of acid-base cycle are shown in Figs. S2 and S3. It can be seen that the UV–Vis absorption of QC-CDs and BP-4 is reversible in both acidic and alkaline conditions. In order to show more intuitively that the UV–Vis absorption of QC-CDs and BP-4 is reversible under acidic and alkaline conditions, we counted the changes in the positions of the absorption peaks of QC-CDs and BP-4 during the acid-base cycle as in Fig. 3b and e. It can be seen that the positions of the absorption peaks of the QC-CDs and BP-4 return to their original locations after each acid-base cycle. The results show that the change of UV absorption of QC-CDs and BP-4 under alkaline conditions is due to deprotonation, rather than the change of structure of QC-CDs and BP-4 under alkaline conditions. This is also illustrated by the FT-IR spectra of QC-CDs and BP-4 under different pH conditions. As shown in Fig. 3c and f, the FT-IR spectra of QC-CDs and BP-4 under acidic and alkaline conditions are the same, indicating that the functional groups on the surface of QC-CDs and BP-4 have not been destroyed by the alkaline conditions. From the reversibility of the UV–Vis absorption of QC-CDs during the acid-base cycle and the absence of changes in the FT-IR spectrum, it is shown that QC-CDs is stable under different pH conditions without destroying the structure and surface functional groups of QC-CDs.

Although the UV absorption of QC-CDs is red-shifted under alkaline conditions, it still completely absorbs ultraviolet. And BP-4 loses its function as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. This also restricts the application of the BP-4. As a result, QC-CDs have a wider range of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV absorption stability and photobleaching resistance of QC-CDs under different temperature conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Since the ultimate purpose of the QC-CDs is to serve as water-soluble UV absorbers, the UV absorption stability of QC-CDs under different temperature conditions and the photobleaching resistance of QC-CDs are also important.

Fig. 4 a shows the UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of QC-CDs under different temperature conditions. It can be seen that the UV absorption of QC-CDs doesn't change under different temperature conditions. As a result, QC-CDs have stable UV absorption at different temperature conditions. The QC-CDs solution was exposed to sunlight and then the UV absorption of the QC-CDs was monitored every 15 days. The results are shown in Fig. 4b, even after the QC-CDs solution was exposed to sunlight for 60 days, the UV absorption of the QC-CDs remains unchanged. Therefore, QC-CDs have good resistance to photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The absorption efficiency of QC-CDs and BP-4
ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of water-soluble UV absorbent, its absorption efficiency is also very important. Therefore, the UV absorption efficiencies of QC-CDs and BP-4 were compared.

The QC-CDs and BP-4 UV absorption efficiency can be calculated by the following formula [4,42]:
(1)
T
Q C − C D s
=
∫ 280 400
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 280 400
d λ
(2)
T
B P − 4
=
∫ 280 400
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 280 400
d λ
(3)
A
Q C − C D s
= 1 −
T
Q C − C D s
(4)
A
B P − 4
= 1 −
T
B P − 4

In the formula,
T λ
is the transmission at λ.
T
Q C − C D s
and
T
B P − 4
are the average transmittances of QC-CDs and BP-4 in the wavelength region of (280–400 nm), respectively.
A
Q C − C D s
and
A
B P − 4
are the absorption efficiency of QC-CDs and BP-4, respectively.

Fig. 5 a shows the UV–Vis transmission spectra of BP-4. Based on Eq. (2), we can calculate the average transmittance of BP-4 at wavelengths of 280–400 nm is 0.34. Fig. 5b shows the UV–Vis transmission spectra of QC-CDs. Based on Eq. (1), we can calculate the average transmittance of QC-CDs at wavelengths of 280–400 nm is 0.01. Based on Eqs. (3) and (4), it can be calculated that the absorption efficiency of QC-CDs is 1.6 times higher than that of BP-4 in the wavelength range of 280–400 nm.
It can be seen that in terms of UV absorption efficiency, QC-CDs is 1.6 times higher than that of BP-4. It is also one of the advantages of QC-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The demonstration of the UV shielding effect of QC-CDs and BP-4
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 a shows the UV–Vis absorption-transmission spectra of QC-CDs and BP-4, it can be seen that QC-CDs can absorb all the UV light and the transmittance in the visible region is also above 95%. However, BP-4 can only absorb the UV light in the region of 200–340 nm, it can't absorb the UV light in the region of 340–400 nm.

Since QC-CDs can absorb UV light with wavelengths of 285 nm and 365 nm, BP-4 can only absorb UV light with a wavelength of 285 nm and can't absorb UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. Therefore, in order to demonstrate the UV shielding effect of BP-4 and QC-CDs more intuitively, we demonstrate this with UV flashlights with wavelengths of 285 nm and 365 nm, respectively. When using the UV flashlight with a wavelength of 285 nm, a model is made as shown in Fig. 6b, a 4-way cuvette was placed between two impervious wooden plates and the cuvette is filled with water, BP-4 and QC-CDs solutions, respectively. Then, the cuvette was irradiated by a UV flashlight with a wavelength of 285 nm. The results are shown in Fig. 6d, it can be seen that when the cuvette contains pure water, the original color of the UV lamp is displayed on the whiteboard behind the cuvette and indicates that pure water can't absorb UV light with a wavelength of 285 nm. However, after changing the water in the cuvette into BP-4 and QC-CDs, the whiteboard behind the cuvette shows a dark shadow image in the shape of a cuvette and indicates that BP-4 and QC-CDs can absorb UV light with a wavelength of 285 nm.

When using the UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm to irradiate a glass bottle filled with BP-4 solution, the result is shown in Fig. 6c, it can be seen that UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm can pass through BP-4 solution, indicating that BP-4 can't absorb UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm. However, when using the UV flashlight with a wavelength of 365 nm to irradiate a glass bottle filled with QC-CDs solution, it can be seen that UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm is blocked at the liquid surface, indicating that QC-CDs can absorb UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm. This demonstrates once again that QC-CDs have a wider range of UV absorption than BP-4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of QC-CDs in UV-absorption systems of waterborne coating
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7 a, the QC-CDs@PU are all highly transparent, and the color of the QC-CDs@PU becomes darker as the concentration of doped QC-CDs increases, but this doesn't affect the high transparency of the films, indicating that the QC-CDs are well dispersed in the PU matrix without obvious aggregation.

Fig. 7b shows the UV–Vis transmission properties of QC-CDs@PU doped with different QC-CDs concentrations, and the UV absorption intensity of QC-CDs@PU increases with the increase of QC-CDs concentration. Based on Eq. (1) and Eq. (3), the UV absorption efficiency of QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs is shown in Fig. S4 and Table S2. When the concentration of QC-CDs increases to 40 mg/mL, the QC-CDs@PU can achieve 98.7% UV absorption efficiency and the transmittance in the visible region is also above 95%. Fig. 7c shows the UV–Vis transmission properties of BP-4@PU doped with different BP-4 concentrations, and the UV absorption intensity of BP-4@PU increases with the increase of BP-4 concentration, too. Based on Eq. (2) and Eq. (4), the UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@PU doped with different concentrations of BP-4 is shown in Fig. S5 and Table S3. The UV absorption efficiencies of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU are summarized in Fig. 7d. When the concentration of BP-4 increases to 40 mg/mL, the BP-4@PU can just achieve 77.6% UV absorption efficiency, while that of QC-CDs@PU can reach 98.7%.

We found that both QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs and BP-4@PU doped with different concentrations of BP-4 could achieve 100% absorption in the UVB band, the main difference between them being in the UVA band (Fig. 7f and g).

The QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU UV absorption efficiency in UVA and UVB can be calculated by the following formula [4,42]:
(5)
T
Q C − C D s @ P U
i n
U V A
=
∫ 320 400
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 320 400
d λ
(6)
T
B P − 4 @ P U
i n
U V A
=
∫ 320 400
T λ
d λ
/
∫ 320 400
d λ
(7)
A
Q C − C D s @ P U
i n
U V A
= 1 −
T
Q C − C D s @ P U
i n
U V A
(8)
A
B P − 4 @ P U
i n
U V A
= 1 −
T
B P − 4 @ P U
i n
U V A

In the formula,
T λ
is the transmission at λ.
T
Q C − C D s @ P U
i n
U V A
and
T
B P − 4 @ P U
i n
U V A
are the average transmittances of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU in the wavelength region of (320–400 nm), respectively.
A
Q C − C D s @ P U
i n
U V A
and
A
B P − 4 @ P U
i n
U V A
are the absorption efficiency of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU, respectively.

Based on Eq. (5) and Eq. (7), the UV absorption efficiency of QC-CDs@PU doped with different concentrations of QC-CDs at UVA and UVB are shown in Fig. S6 and Table 1 . Based on Eq. (6) and Eq. (8), the UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@PU doped with different concentrations of BP-4 at UVA and UVB are shown in Fig. S7 and Table 2 . The absorption efficiencies of QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU in UVA and UVB bands are summarized in Fig. 7e. When the concentration of BP-4 is 40 mg/mL, the UV absorption efficiency of BP-4@PU in the UVA band is only 66.4%, while that of QC-CDs@PU can reach 98.2%.

QC-CDs were successfully applied in UV absorption systems of waterborne coatings PU. The addition of QC-CDs not only does not affect the original properties of the PU, but also endows it with full UV absorption properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of QC-CDs@PU in preventing photodegradation of rhodamine B
ce:section.ce:para: Rhodamine B is an organic dye that will fade under continuous UV irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the rhodamine B solution was wrapped by 40 mg/mL QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU under continuous 365 UV irradiation, respectively. The absorption spectra of rhodamine B solution under different UV irradiation times were tested by using a UV spectrophotometer Fig. 8(b-e), which showed that the absorption intensity of rhodamine B solution wrapped by QC-CDs@PU hardly changed under continuous UV irradiation (Fig. 8b), while the absorption intensity of rhodamine B solution wrapped by BP-4@PU decreased significantly under continuous UV irradiation (Fig. 8d), indicating that part of rhodamine B had been photodegraded. The degradation rates of rhodamine B wrapped by QC-CDs@PU and BP-4@PU under different UV irradiation times were counted in Fig. 8e. It can be seen that after 192 h of continuous UV irradiation, the rhodamine B solution wrapped by BP-4@PU had degraded by 50%, while the rhodamine B solution wrapped by QC-CDs@PU only a slight decrease. The physical drawing is shown in Fig. S8. It shows that QC-CDs@PU has a good UV shielding effect.
QC-CDs were successfully applied in UV absorption systems of waterborne coatings PU, which enables PU to completely absorb UV.

In summary, a full wavelength of UV-absorbing QC-CDs was prepared as UV absorbers by a one-step hydrothermal method using quercetin as raw material. By comparing the UV–Vis absorption spectra of quercetin and QC-CDs and combing the simple infrared spectra of QC-CDs, the UV absorption mechanism of QC-CDs was obtained. The absorption peak at 261 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ leap of the benzene ring C=C bond attached to the surface of the carbon core. Compared to the π-π∗ leap of the benzene ring C=C in quercetin, it has some red shift, which is caused by the large π-conjugation of carbon core. While the absorption peak at 290 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ leap of the aromatic cluster attached to the surface of the carbon core, and the absorption peak at 362 nm is attributed to the n-π∗ leap of the C=O bond. Experiments reveal that QC-CDs have good resistance to photobleaching and stable UV absorption at different temperatures. Comparing QC-CDs with the commercial aqueous UV absorber BP-4, it was found that QC-CDs not only have a wider UV-absorption range, but also can completely absorb UV light under acid and alkaline conditions. However, BP-4 loses its value as a UV absorber under alkaline conditions. In terms of aging resistance, QC-CDs can achieve the same results as BP-4. Finally, QC-CDs were applied to the UV absorption system of waterborne coating PU and make PU completely absorb UV. At present, the preparation process of QC-CDs is simple, low cost, safe and environmentally friendly. So, QC-CDs have the potential to become a new generation of water-soluble UV absorbers.

Zhiqiang Xu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Visualization, Writing original draft. Wanyi Huang: Methodology, Data curation. Congcong Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Weihao Ye: Data curation, Formal analysis. Baoyan Guo: Data curation. Jiemin Qiu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Jianle Zhuang: Data curation. Chaofan Hu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Bingfu Lei: Data curation, Formal analysis. Guangqi Hu: Funding acquisition. Yingliang Liu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
